topic: reflecting on year — team change, coming out, new relationship

turns.content: I have been thinking about the past 18 months or so. Not in a sentimental way. More like a retrospective analysis. A lot has changed and I want to understand the shape of it.
That sounds like a useful exercise. What's prompting the retrospective — is it a specific milestone, or more of a general sense that things look different now?

General pattern recognition, I think. I was looking at some old notes and realized that the version of me from 18 months ago would not recognize several of the current variables in my life. My team changed. My relationship changed entirely. My family structure changed. Even my daily routine is different. If I plotted it on a chart, it would not be a smooth line. It would be a series of step changes.
Step changes rather than a gradual slope — that's an interesting way to see it. Does that pattern surprise you? Or looking back, can you see how each step led to the next?

Some of them were connected. The work change led to reconsidering other things. When one part of your life shifts, it destabilizes the equilibrium and you start questioning whether the other parts are where they should be. But some changes were independent. My brother having a baby was not related to anything I did. It just happened and now I am an aunt, which is a permanent state change. No rollback possible.

The equilibrium metaphor is apt. One shift raises the threshold for inertia everywhere else. It sounds like the work change was a catalyst that made you more willing to let other things be uncertain for a while. Is that how it felt at the time, or is that only clear in retrospect?

Only clear in retrospect, honestly. At the time I was mostly annoyed. I do not like change that I did not initiate. But once the first change happened and I survived it, the subsequent ones felt less threatening. There is probably a psychological term for that. Exposure effect or adaptation or something.

It's sometimes called psychological flexibility — the capacity to adapt to change increases with practice. Each experience of surviving an unwanted change builds evidence that you can handle the next one. It sounds like the involuntary change at work paradoxically made you braver about the voluntary changes in your personal life.

That is actually a precise description of what happened. I would not have used the word brave, but the mechanism is correct. And the personal changes — they were the ones that mattered more. Coming out, ending a long relationship, starting a new one. Those were significantly harder than switching teams. The work change was a process. The personal changes required something I am not naturally good at, which is acting without complete information.

Acting without complete information — that's a real tension for someone who values data the way you do. Those personal decisions didn't have a spreadsheet that could resolve them. How do you feel about the choices you made, now that you're on the other side?

If I am being analytical about it, which is the only way I know how to be, the outcomes have been positive across all dimensions that I can measure. My mood data is better. My energy is higher. I am more honest with the people around me. The relationship I am in now feels qualitatively different in a way that is hard to capture numerically, which I have decided to interpret as a good sign rather than a data gap. But I also recognize that I am in what a statistician would call a local maximum. I do not know if this is the global maximum. I probably never will. That used to bother me. It bothers me less now.

The shift from needing to find the global maximum to accepting a local one that feels right — that might be the most significant change in the whole retrospective. It sounds like you're learning to tolerate uncertainty in the parts of life where certainty isn't available, without abandoning your analytical nature in the parts where it is.

That is a lagom summary, actually. I have not abandoned any of my frameworks. I still track everything. I still want the data. But I have added a new category to my decision-making model, which I would label 'felt sense, unquantified.' It has a lower confidence interval than my other inputs but it has a surprisingly good track record over the past year. I am learning to weight it appropriately.

Felt sense, unquantified — with a surprisingly good track record. That sounds like an important addition to the model. Not every analyst gets there. The fact that you can incorporate it without discarding the rest of your framework is a kind of integration that takes real intellectual honesty. It has been a substantial 18 months.
